pillars.name: Booth Identity and Setup Taste
postIdeas: generic white tent vs a booth that actually looks like a brand
what your canopy setup says before anyone reads the sign
why polished branding feels more trustworthy at a market or expo
the first thing people judge when they walk an event row
taste matters more than most booth owners want to admit
pillars.examplePost: People say they are judging the product. Half the time they are judging the setup first and deciding whether the brand looks serious enough to stop for.
contextHints: Lead with identity, taste, and first impressions instead of shopping language.
The post should feel like a booth opinion from someone who has seen a lot of bad setups.

pillars.name: Vendor Life and Event Culture
postIdeas: 5 a.m. load-in energy before a market opens
the chaos of setup when wind shows up before the customers do
how real vendors think about booth flow during a packed event
the little event-day rituals only market people understand
why some booths feel alive before the first customer even arrives
pillars.examplePost: Vendor life is loading the van before sunrise, fixing one weird problem in the parking lot, then acting normal by 9 a.m. like none of that happened.
contextHints: Write like someone who has actually set up in a parking lot, market row, or festival field.
Make the post relatable enough that a vendor would send it to another vendor.

pillars.name: Utah Event Radar
postIdeas: upcoming Utah markets, fairs, trade shows, and festivals worth watching
what kinds of booth setups fit spring, summer, and fall events in Utah
outdoor event season in Salt Lake, Utah County, and southern Utah
how event calendars create last-minute booth panic without saying buy now
the difference between indoor expo energy and outdoor festival energy
pillars.examplePost: Utah event season sneaks up the same way every year. One week everybody is talking spring, the next week half the vendor world is realizing their setup still looks like last season.
contextHints: Be useful to people who care about upcoming local events, not just products.
Treat the event itself as the main story and the booth setup as the subtext.

pillars.name: Booth Proof in the Wild
postIdeas: what wind, sun, and repeated setup reveal in real life
why some booths still look sharp at hour eight and others look exhausted by noon
details that quietly make a booth feel legit
what event people notice about frames, fabric, and print quality without naming it
proof that the setup matters without sounding like a spec sheet
pillars.examplePost: Nobody at an event says nice frame joints. They just notice which booth still looks put together after weather, foot traffic, and three bad setup decisions.
contextHints: Use proof and event reality, not spec-sheet bragging.
The canopy can be visible in the scene without becoming a hard pitch.

pillars.name: Hot Takes and Booth Glow-Ups
postIdeas: hot take: your booth setup is the first review your business gets
before and after booth identity without saying before and after
why some booths look handmade in a good way and others look random
opinions about tablecloth booths, droopy banners, and visual clutter
customer setup features that feel aspirational instead of promotional
pillars.examplePost: Hot take. A lot of booth problems are not product problems. They are taste problems wearing a folding table and three conflicting signs.
contextHints: Let the post have a real opinion. Bland never gets shared.
Aim for screenshot-worthy booth commentary, not sales copy.
